one_line_takeaway: A secure-by-design position paper: it recasts LLM-agent memory poisoning as classical integrity/provenance/PIR problems, backs the claim with a two-knowledge-base PIR Prolog inference proof-of-concept, and admits that interaction-driven poisoning of dynamic memory remains unformalized and unsolved.
key_finding: Many alleged semantic-memory poisoning attacks (which assume a known clean database Dclean and injected adversarial key-value pairs) dissolve under standard integrity/provenance controls: hashing detects unauthorized changes, signatures bind provenance, and encryption keeps Dclean hidden even on third-party retrieval services, undermining the attacker's optimization. For the harder untrusted-KB setting, a working two-KB PIR-backed Horn-clause inference engine lets an agent obtain rules/facts without revealing its query. Crucially, the authors identify that dynamic, interaction-based poisoning has NO static Dclean to protect and cannot be captured by the AgentPoison-style formalism, and that no private-inference solution exists in the literature for knowledge-base inference (FHE private inference is practical only for small neural nets).
core_contribution: A position/conceptual paper (Torra & Bras-Amoros) that argues memory poisoning in LLM-based agents and multi-agent systems (MAS) can, in large part, be countered by 'secure-by-design' agents built from established security and cryptographic primitives rather than only ML-side defenses. Its own contributions are: (1) a taxonomy of agent memory into semantic, episodic, and short-term memory (distinguished by duration/consolidation), with per-type analysis of the feasibility of poisoning; (2) a matched set of proposed mitigations per memory type (hashing to detect tampering, digital signatures for provenance, data-provenance structures, append-only/immutable hash-chain memory, secure communication, and trust/reputation mechanisms); and (3) an actual implemented proof-of-concept: a Python Prolog-like (Horn-clause) local inference engine that retrieves rules/facts from external, untrusted knowledge bases via Private Information Retrieval (PIR), so an untrusted server cannot learn which knowledge is queried. It also flags interaction-driven poisoning of dynamic memory as an open, under-formalized problem.
threat_model: A malicious agent purposely modifies another agent's memory (semantic, episodic, or short-term) to change the victim agent's behavior toward a harmful action (e.g., deleting a home directory, buying a wrong flight ticket). Attack surfaces include: third-party/untrusted or unverified knowledge bases hosting the memory; direct tampering of stored episodic/short-term memory; propagation of triggers from short-term into consolidated episodic memory; manipulation of sensors/actuators feeding the world model; and stealthy multi-turn agent interactions that inject harmful content through ostensibly benign exchanges. The paper distinguishes this from prompt hacking (injection/jailbreaking), where the goal is prompt-induced misbehavior with no intended write access to memory.
limitations: Proof-of-concept only, with no quantitative evaluation of correctness, latency, robustness, or attack resistance. The single-server k-anonymity variant explicitly offers weaker (non-information-theoretic) privacy than the two-server PIR. Private inference for knowledge-base reasoning is unsolved and FHE-based private inference is limited to small models. The paper concedes that interaction-based poisoning of dynamic memory is 'difficult to formalize and solve' and that it is 'unclear how these interactions can affect future behavior, except in unrealistic cases.' Secure episodic-memory update/consolidation algorithms are named as an open research question. Much of the mitigation discussion is prescriptive (what should be done) rather than demonstrated.

datasets_used: No standard datasets or benchmarks; evaluation is illustrative only

Toy Horn-clause knowledge base of 6 logical expressions (E1-E6) contributed by agents A, B, C (e.g., man(socrates), woman(hypathia), man=>mortal, woman=>mortal, car/bicycle=>vehicle), used to demonstrate the PIR inference engine on the query mortal(hypathia)
quantitative_results: Two-KB PIR cost: for a knowledge base with n keys, the agent sends n bits to each of the 2 servers and receives r bits from each, where r is the length of the padded value (facts+rules) for a key
Generalized 2^d-database PIR (cited comparison): sends d*n^(1/d) bits total and receives r bits from each database, XOR-ing d*r bits
Single-server k-anonymity variant: submits k indices and retrieves k*r bits, from which the local engine filters the target knowledge

Engine constraints: recursion permitted, Prolog cut (!) not implemented, returns all solutions up to a maximum recursion depth; KB keyed by (predicate name, arity) with padding to equalize index lengths
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines. Conceptual/cost contrasts only: the authors' 2-KB PIR protocol vs. the 2^d-database PIR scheme (lower per-message bit cost via d*n^(1/d)), and vs. the lighter single-server k-anonymity variant (weaker privacy, lower cost)

The AgentPoison attack model (Chen et al., NeurIPS 2024) and the Minja query-only injection attack (Dong et al., NeurIPS 2025) are reviewed as cited prior work, not as the authors' own baselines or results
